questions.q: What is Clean Architecture (Onion Architecture), and what is the Dependency Rule?

answer.oneLiner: Concentric rings — Domain at the centre, then Application, then Infrastructure and Presentation — where all dependencies point inward and the domain knows nothing about the outside.
keyPoints: Domain (centre): entities, value objects, zero dependencies.
Application: use-cases and interfaces like IRepository.
Infrastructure/Presentation (outer): EF, HTTP, controllers.
Dependency Rule: source dependencies point inward only — via Dependency Inversion.
answer.detail: Interfaces are defined in inner layers and implemented in outer ones, so you can swap DB, broker, or UI without touching domain code. Use-cases test in milliseconds against fakes.

code.note: The Application layer defines IOrderRepository; Infrastructure implements it. Dependency flows inward.

answer.analogy: Like an onion — peel any outer layer and the core business rules stay intact.
followUps: How does it differ from traditional N-Tier?
What role does MediatR play here?
How do you handle cross-cutting concerns without polluting the domain?
redFlags: Defining repositories in Infrastructure and calling them from Application.
Putting business logic in controllers or EF entities.
Treating it as 'just folders' without enforcing the dependency rule.

questions.q: Explain the Repository Pattern — what it is, why you use it, and the criticisms when used with Entity Framework.

answer.oneLiner: It wraps data access behind a collection-like interface so callers don't care where data comes from — but with EF Core it can be redundant since DbContext is already a unit-of-work plus repository.
keyPoints: Centralises query logic and enables testing against fakes.
Criticism: EF's DbSet is already a repository, DbContext a UoW.
Returning IQueryable leaks EF; returning IEnumerable forces in-memory filtering.
Prefer specific repos (IOrderRepository) over generic IRepository<T>.
answer.detail: A pragmatic middle ground is query/specification objects run directly against DbContext — testable without the leaky wrapper. It genuinely pays off with multiple data sources or strict Clean Architecture boundaries.

code.note: Specific repositories communicate domain language; generic IQueryable-based ones leak infrastructure concerns.

answer.analogy: Like a librarian — you ask for 'Tolkien books after 1960' and get a list, not access to the storeroom.
followUps: What is the Specification Pattern?
Generic vs specific repository — which and why?
How do you test data access without a real DB?
redFlags: Saying repositories are always needed with EF.
Returning IQueryable from an Application-layer repository.
Never having heard the criticism.

questions.q: What is the Unit of Work pattern, and how does EF Core implement it?

answer.oneLiner: Unit of Work tracks all changes during a business operation and commits them atomically — EF Core's DbContext is the canonical implementation.
keyPoints: Collects inserts/updates/deletes, then one SaveChanges in a single transaction.
DbContext is a UoW: ChangeTracker records, SaveChangesAsync commits.
Repositories in one request share the same scoped DbContext.
An explicit IUnitOfWork lets Application control commit timing without exposing EF.
answer.detail: Commit once at the use-case boundary — multiple independent SaveChanges calls cause partial commits. Keep the UoW scoped tightly to avoid stale data and long-held connections.

code.note: Single CommitAsync at the use-case boundary ensures order + inventory change in one transaction.

followUps: How do you handle transactions across microservices?
IDbContextTransaction vs TransactionScope?
Why register DbContext as Scoped, not Singleton?
redFlags: Calling SaveChangesAsync inside the repository, not at the boundary.
Registering DbContext as Singleton.
Confusing Unit of Work with Repository.

questions.q: What is CQRS and when should you use it?

answer.oneLiner: CQRS separates writes (Commands) from reads (Queries) so each side can be optimised and scaled independently.
keyPoints: Commands mutate and return little; Queries return data, never mutate.
Reads can hit a denormalised model, cache, or replica — bypassing domain rules.
Writes go through full domain validation and aggregates.
MediatR dispatches commands/queries; event sourcing is optional and complex.
answer.detail: Useful when reads dwarf writes or domain logic is complex. Skip it for simple CRUD — it just adds ceremony.

code.note: Query handler bypasses repository and domain layer entirely — reads directly from DbContext for performance.

answer.analogy: A kitchen: orders go to the chef (commands); the menu board is just read (queries).
followUps: How does eventual consistency relate to a separate read store?
How do you handle validation in a CQRS pipeline?
CQRS vs Event Sourcing — must they go together?
redFlags: Thinking CQRS always needs a separate DB or event sourcing.
Returning full entities from Query handlers.
Applying it to simple CRUD without justification.

questions.q: Monolith vs Microservices — how do you choose, and what are the real trade-offs?

answer.oneLiner: Monoliths are simpler to build and run; microservices give independent scaling and deployment but add distributed-system complexity — choose on team size, domain maturity, and operational capability.
keyPoints: Monolith: one deploy, easy debugging, ACID across the domain.
Microservices: independent deploy, fault isolation, targeted scaling.
Costs: network calls, eventual consistency, observability overhead.
Start with a modular monolith; extract services only with a proven reason.
answer.detail: It's really an operational-capability and domain-complexity question. If you can't run Kubernetes, tracing, and multi-service CI/CD, microservices hurt more than they help.
followUps: Modular Monolith vs Big Ball of Mud?
How do you handle cross-service transactions?
What is the Strangler Fig pattern?
redFlags: Saying microservices are always better.
Can't name the pain they introduce.
Never heard of the modular monolith.

questions.q: How do you design for scalability and fault tolerance? Explain horizontal vs vertical scaling, retries, and the circuit breaker pattern.

answer.oneLiner: Scale out behind a load balancer with stateless services, add retries with backoff and circuit breakers to stop cascades — in .NET, wire it with Polly or the standard resilience handler.
keyPoints: Vertical = bigger box (ceiling); horizontal = more instances (needs stateless).
Stateless: session/cache in Redis, not in-process.
Retry with exponential backoff + jitter for transient failures.
Circuit breaker fails fast after N failures; always add timeouts.
answer.detail: Circuit breaker states: Closed (normal), Open (fail fast), Half-Open (probe). Order matters: timeout wraps circuit breaker wraps retry. Bulkheads cap concurrent calls so a slow dependency can't exhaust shared resources.

code.note: Order matters: timeout wraps circuit breaker wraps retry — innermost policy executes first.

answer.analogy: A circuit breaker is like a fuse — it trips to protect the system instead of letting the wiring burn.
followUps: Retry vs idempotency key?
How do you add a health check in ASP.NET Core?
What is the Bulkhead pattern?
redFlags: Retrying non-idempotent POSTs without idempotency keys.
No jitter on retries — thundering herd.
Retries without a circuit breaker — amplifies load.

questions.q: What is an API Gateway and how do services communicate with each other in a microservices architecture?

answer.oneLiner: An API Gateway is the single entry point handling routing, auth, rate limiting, and SSL; services talk internally via sync HTTP/gRPC or async messaging depending on coupling.
keyPoints: Gateway: routing, JWT validation, rate limiting, SSL, aggregation.
Azure options: APIM, Application Gateway, or YARP in-process.
Sync (HTTP/gRPC) when caller needs an answer now; gRPC for internal calls.
Async (Service Bus/Kafka) to decouple services from each other's uptime.
answer.detail: A BFF is a gateway tailored per client type. Watch the chatty anti-pattern — synchronous A→B→C chains stack latency; prefer events for non-critical paths.
followUps: API Gateway vs Service Mesh?
How do you secure service-to-service calls (mTLS)?
YARP vs Azure APIM — when each?
redFlags: Saying HTTP is always right without considering async.
Forgetting the gateway is a single point of failure.
Conflating an API Gateway with a load balancer.

questions.q: What is CORS, and how does ASP.NET Core handle it? How does it relate to event-driven design?

answer.oneLiner: CORS is a browser mechanism that blocks cross-origin requests unless the server allows them via response headers — ASP.NET Core configures this with built-in middleware.
keyPoints: Browsers enforce same-origin (scheme + domain + port) by default.
Server sends Access-Control-Allow-* headers to permit cross-origin calls.
Non-simple requests trigger a preflight OPTIONS request.
Configure with AddCors + UseCors; never wildcard with credentials.
answer.detail: CORS only affects browser-to-server calls, not server-to-server. In event-driven setups, backend consumers use a broker so CORS never applies — only the BFF/gateway needs it.

code.note: Always specify explicit origins in production — never use AllowAnyOrigin() with AllowCredentials().

followUps: Simple request vs preflight?
How do you handle CORS in Azure APIM?
Why doesn't CORS protect against CSRF?
redFlags: AllowAnyOrigin() with AllowCredentials() — rejected by browsers.
Thinking CORS protects server-to-server calls.
Placing UseCors after UseAuthorization.

questions.q: Compare Layered (N-Tier) Architecture with Clean Architecture — when would you choose each?

answer.oneLiner: N-Tier flows top-down (UI → Logic → Data) with higher layers depending on lower; Clean Architecture inverts it so the domain depends on nothing — pick based on size and testability needs.
keyPoints: N-Tier: business layer references concrete data-access classes.
Clean: domain defines interfaces, infrastructure implements them.
N-Tier is simpler — fine for small CRUD apps.
Clean is testable in isolation but adds projects and ceremony.
answer.detail: The key difference is where interfaces live. Small app with one dev → N-Tier. Domain-rich, larger team, long horizon → Clean pays off. For brownfield, introduce interfaces gradually.
followUps: What is Vertical Slice Architecture?
Can you mix N-Tier and Clean in one solution?
What is the Application layer's role in Clean?
redFlags: Thinking Clean is always superior.
Describing N-Tier without the downward dependency direction.
Not knowing the difference is where interfaces are defined.

questions.q: What is the Mediator Pattern? How does MediatR implement it in .NET, and what are its benefits and downsides?

answer.oneLiner: The Mediator routes messages through a central hub so senders and receivers don't know each other; MediatR dispatches Command/Query objects to handlers and adds pipeline behaviours for cross-cutting concerns.
keyPoints: Decouples components — a controller sends a command, not a class call.
IMediator.Send routes to one IRequestHandler; DI discovers handlers.
Pipeline behaviours add validation, logging, caching composably.
Downsides: hides dependencies, and it's overkill for simple CRUD.
answer.detail: The main value beyond dispatch is IPipelineBehavior — validation, logging, transactions run before handlers without them knowing. For 3-5 endpoints, direct service injection is cleaner.

code.note: Controller depends only on IMediator — completely decoupled from the handler implementation.

followUps: How do you register pipeline behaviours in .NET 8?
IRequest vs INotification?
How would you build a caching behaviour?
redFlags: Saying MediatR is CQRS — it only facilitates it.
Not knowing what IPipelineBehavior does.
Using MediatR with no pipeline behaviours.

questions.q: What are the core concepts of Domain-Driven Design (DDD) relevant to a senior .NET developer?

answer.oneLiner: DDD structures software around the business domain using Bounded Contexts, Aggregates, Entities, Value Objects, and Domain Events, with a shared Ubiquitous Language.
keyPoints: Ubiquitous Language: same terms in code, tests, and conversations.
Bounded Context: a boundary where one model is consistent.
Aggregate Root enforces invariants; all changes flow through it.
Entity has identity; Value Object is immutable and identity-less.
answer.detail: Aggregates reference others by ID, stay small, and raise domain events dispatched after SaveChanges. Each microservice is ideally one bounded context. Best for complex domains, not CRUD.

code.note: Aggregate root encapsulates invariants; only it can raise domain events. Child entities (OrderLine) are not accessible for direct modification externally.

followUps: How do you implement domain events with EF Core and MediatR?
What is an Anti-Corruption Layer?
How do you model a bounded context boundary in a .NET solution?
redFlags: Treating every table as an aggregate.
Not knowing Entity vs Value Object.
Using tactical patterns without the strategic ones.

questions.q: What is the SAGA pattern for distributed transactions, and how does it differ from two-phase commit?

answer.oneLiner: A SAGA is a sequence of local transactions where each step triggers the next via events; if one fails, compensating transactions undo prior steps — eventual consistency without a distributed lock.
keyPoints: 2PC locks resources across services — strong consistency but a distributed lock.
SAGA: each service does a local transaction and emits an event.
Compensating transactions undo earlier steps and must be idempotent.
Choreography (no coordinator) vs orchestration (central controller).
answer.detail: Orchestrated SAGAs map naturally to Azure Durable Functions. Idempotency is mandatory because retries replay messages. The system is temporarily inconsistent, so the UI must show 'processing'.
followUps: How do you implement idempotency in a SAGA step?
Choreography vs orchestration?
How do Durable Functions help?
redFlags: Suggesting 2PC as the default in microservices.
Not knowing what a compensating transaction is.
Thinking eventual consistency means 'consistent at some random time'.

questions.q: Synchronous vs asynchronous communication in microservices — how do you decide?

answer.oneLiner: Use sync (HTTP/gRPC) when the caller needs an immediate result; use async messaging when work can be deferred or services should be decoupled from each other's availability.
keyPoints: Sync: simple and traceable but couples availability.
Async: broker buffers spikes and enables retry without the caller.
Sync hops stack latency; async defers it.
Always use DLQs for messages that repeatedly fail.
answer.detail: Use async for events, notifications, and cross-context integration; sync for real-time queries and auth. Watch the dual-write problem — solve it with the Transactional Outbox or CDC.
followUps: What is the Transactional Outbox Pattern?
How do you guarantee ordering in Service Bus?
Queue vs topic in Service Bus?
redFlags: Defaulting to async for everything.
Not knowing the dual-write problem.
Treating async as 'fire and forget' without retry/DLQ/idempotency.

questions.q: What is idempotency in distributed systems, and how do you implement it in a .NET API?

answer.oneLiner: An operation is idempotent if running it many times gives the same result as running it once — essential when retries and at-least-once delivery are the norm.
keyPoints: GET/PUT/DELETE are idempotent by spec; POST is not.
Idempotency key: client sends a UUID; API returns the stored result on replay.
Store keys in Redis with TTL or a dedicated table.
Consumers check a processed message ID before doing work.
answer.detail: A POST that times out and retries can double-charge unless an idempotency key dedupes it. Use Redis SET ... NX atomically to handle simultaneous duplicate requests.

code.note: Middleware approach applies idempotency across all POST endpoints without handler-level code.

followUps: How do you handle concurrent duplicate requests?
Idempotency vs at-most-once / at-least-once delivery?
How does Service Bus do duplicate detection?
redFlags: Thinking GET requests need idempotency keys.
Storing keys without considering atomicity with the resource.
Not giving idempotency keys a TTL.

questions.q: How do you decide on a project's architecture and key design decisions?

answer.oneLiner: Treat it as trade-off analysis: nail the quality attributes and constraints, pick the simplest architecture that meets them, and capture the decision in an ADR.
keyPoints: Gather quality attributes first — read/write heavy, latency, availability.
Know your constraints: team skill, budget, infra, compliance.
Start simple — usually a modular monolith.
Document each decision as an ADR and revisit periodically.
answer.detail: Sketch 2-3 candidate architectures, stress-test them against the NFRs, and present the trade-offs honestly — don't hide the cost of microservices. YAGNI applies to architecture too.
followUps: How do you get stakeholder buy-in when they resist?
What is the Strangler Fig pattern?
How do you tell when an architecture needs to change?
redFlags: Choosing microservices 'because everyone does'.
Deciding alone without documenting reasoning.
Never heard of ADRs.

questions.q: Describe a significant technical challenge you faced and how you resolved it.

answer.oneLiner: Use STAR with technical depth — set the context, state your specific role, spend most of the answer on your reasoning and options, then quantify the result.
keyPoints: Situation: system, scale, team, business pressure — briefly.
Task: your specific responsibility.
Action (the bulk): options considered, what you chose and why.
Result: quantify, and note what you'd do differently.
answer.detail: Worked example: an Azure Functions pipeline silently dropped ~0.3% of orders. Diagnosed via App Insights and correlation IDs that the Service Bus lock expired before processing finished; chose lock renewal as the immediate fix and scheduled the async refactor. Result: zero lost orders next peak.
followUps: What did you learn that changed your approach?
How did you communicate risk to non-technical stakeholders?
What would you do differently?
redFlags: Vague: 'I optimised the database' with no specifics.
Not quantifying the result.
No clarity on your own contribution.
No alternatives considered.

questions.q: How do you frame an answer about a performance improvement you've implemented?

answer.oneLiner: Baseline measurement → bottleneck found by profiling (not guessing) → solution with trade-offs explained → measured improvement.
keyPoints: Start with a baseline — p50/p95/p99, throughput, CPU.
Find the bottleneck with tools: App Insights, dotTrace, Query Store.
Common .NET wins: N+1, missing indexes, sync blocking, over-loading.
Quantify: 'p95 from 1.8s to 220ms', 'DB CPU 80% to 30%'.
answer.detail: Worked example: listing API at ~2s p95. Found an N+1, a missing composite index, and over-eager entity loading; fixed with Include, the index, and a DTO projection — p95 dropped to ~190ms. Acknowledge cache staleness as a trade-off.
followUps: How do you spot an N+1 in production safely?
Eager vs lazy vs explicit loading in EF Core?
Cache vs optimise the query — how to decide?
redFlags: 'I added some indexes and it got faster' — no methodology.
Can't name the profiling tool used.
Ignoring trade-offs like cache invalidation.

questions.q: How do you handle a production incident? Walk me through your process.

answer.oneLiner: Four phases: detect via monitoring, mitigate to restore service first, root-cause it, then prevent recurrence with a blameless post-mortem.
keyPoints: Detect: alerts before customers — error rate, p95, dependency failures.
Mitigate first: roll back a suspect deploy before deep debugging.
Root cause: 5 Whys and a timeline from logs/traces, no blame.
Prevent: code fix plus tests, alerts, and runbook updates.
answer.detail: Example: SQL DTU at 100% after a 01:40 deploy introduced an unindexed query; roll back restores service in minutes, then add the index, a CI query-perf test, and a 70% DTU alert. Keep stakeholders updated every 10-15 minutes via one channel.
followUps: What is a blameless post-mortem and why does it matter?
Symptom vs root cause in RCA?
How do you avoid alert fatigue?
redFlags: Debugging root cause before mitigating impact.
Not writing a post-mortem.
'We fixed it and moved on' — no prevention.
Blaming a colleague.

questions.q: An API's response time jumped from 200ms to 3 seconds overnight. Walk me through how you would debug it.

answer.oneLiner: Isolate the layer with data: check recent deployments, read traces to see where time goes, then drill into SQL plans, app code, or external dependencies.
keyPoints: Check deployment and infra history first.
Use a distributed trace to find the slow span.
SQL slow? Query Store, missing indexes, parameter sniffing, blocking.
App slow? Sync blocking, thread-pool starvation, GC pressure.
answer.detail: Confirm scope (one endpoint or all, intermittent or constant), correlate with changes, then expand a slow trace. A single 2.8s SQL span points to a query; many small calls point to an N+1. Also check if a dataset crossed a tipping point.

code.snippet:
SELECT TOP 10 qs.total_elapsed_time / qs.execution_count AS avg_elapsed_time,
       qs.execution_count, qt.text
FROM sys.dm_exec_query_stats qs
CROSS APPLY sys.dm_exec_sql_text(qs.sql_handle) qt
ORDER BY avg_elapsed_time DESC;
code.note: Top queries by average elapsed time; compare against Query Store history to find a newly regressed query.

followUps: What is parameter sniffing and how do you fix it?
How do you detect thread pool starvation?
When use App Insights Live Metrics over the portal?
redFlags: Restarting the server without capturing diagnostics.
'I'd add logging' without saying what or why.
Not checking recent deployments.
Only blaming the DB without checking the app.

questions.q: How would you design a notification system (email, SMS, push) for a large-scale .NET application?

answer.oneLiner: An async, channel-agnostic pipeline: producers publish notification-intent events; a notification service applies user preferences and routes to channel handlers with retry, dedup, and audit.
keyPoints: Producers emit domain events, not 'send email' commands.
Service Bus topic with per-channel subscriptions (email/SMS/push).
Notification service: preferences, templates, rate limiting, dedup.
Retry with backoff, DLQ on failure, and audit every attempt.
answer.detail: Abstract providers (ACS, SendGrid, Twilio, FCM) behind INotificationChannel so they're swappable. Use MessageId as the idempotency key, and scale out with competing consumers.
followUps: How do you handle GDPR/CAN-SPAM opt-out?
How do you handle stale push device tokens?
When use Azure Notification Hubs over direct FCM/APNs?
redFlags: Sending notifications synchronously in the request handler.
Hard-coding channel logic in the business service.
No user preferences or GDPR compliance.
No retry or DLQ — silent failures.

questions.q: How do you decide on a caching strategy for a .NET application?

answer.oneLiner: Pick the layer by freshness, access pattern, and sharing needs — in-process for single-instance hot data, Redis for distributed, CDN for static — and define invalidation before adding any cache.
keyPoints: Layers: CDN → response cache → IMemoryCache → Redis → DB.
Cache-Aside is the default: miss → load → store → return.
TTL is simplest; event-driven invalidation is precise but complex.
Guard against stampede with a lock or stale-while-revalidate.
answer.detail: Use Redis for per-user data across instances, IMemoryCache for single-instance aggregates. Don't cache always-fresh data (financial totals) — optimise the query instead. Never cache user data under a shared key.

code.note: Cache-Aside with explicit invalidation on write. Use IDistributedCache (Redis) for multi-instance scenarios.

followUps: IMemoryCache vs IDistributedCache?
How do you handle cache stampede?
Write-Through vs Cache-Aside?
redFlags: Caching everything without a TTL or invalidation strategy.
'Just use Redis' without considering sharing or freshness.
Ignoring the double-write consistency problem.
Caching user data under a shared key.

questions.q: How do you handle a memory leak discovered in a production .NET application?

answer.oneLiner: Confirm the trend, capture a dump in production, find the largest object graphs and their retention path with a heap profiler, then fix the root cause.
keyPoints: Real leak = working set grows over hours with no GC recovery.
dotnet-counters to watch gc-heap-size and time-in-gc.
dotnet-dump collect, then analyze with dumpheap and gcroot.
Common causes: static collections, unsubscribed events, undisposed IDisposables.
answer.detail: gcroot shows what's keeping objects alive — often a static dictionary with no eviction. Fixes: IMemoryCache with limits, unsubscribe in Dispose, use IHttpClientFactory, fix Singleton-capturing-Scoped. Validate by watching memory plateau over 24h.

code.snippet:
dotnet-dump analyze /tmp/dump.dmp
> dumpheap -stat                  # top types by total bytes
> dumpheap -type System.String   # how many strings are alive
> gcroot <object-address>         # what's keeping this object alive?
code.note: gcroot reveals the chain from a GC root (static field, thread stack) to the leaking object.

followUps: Memory leak vs high memory usage?
How do you detect a Singleton capturing a Scoped DbContext?
PerfView vs dotnet-dump?
redFlags: Restarting on a schedule to 'fix' the leak.
Not capturing a dump before restarting.
Blaming the GC without checking the heap.
Not knowing why Gen2 growth indicates a true leak.

questions.q: How do you scale a read-heavy API that is approaching its capacity limit?

answer.oneLiner: Scale in cheapest-first layers: cache (response/Redis/CDN), then a DB read replica, then horizontal API scaling — each multiplies capacity without expensive vertical scaling.
keyPoints: Profile first — the top 20% of endpoints drive most traffic.
CDN/response caching for public responses; Redis for per-user data.
DB read replica to offload SELECT traffic.
Stateless API + autoscale; CQRS read models for complex views.
answer.detail: Escalation ladder: CDN → IMemoryCache → Redis → read replica → horizontal scaling → pre-materialised read model. Remember replicas have lag, so reads after writes can be stale.

code.note: Use AsNoTracking and a read-replica connection for read queries. Azure SQL supports ApplicationIntent=ReadOnly.

followUps: How do you invalidate cache when the write side changes?
When would you choose Azure SQL Hyperscale?
How do you avoid stale reads for time-sensitive queries?
redFlags: Jumping straight to 'add more servers'.
Not knowing read replicas have replication lag.
Adding Redis without a TTL or invalidation strategy.

questions.q: Explain DDD building blocks: aggregates, entities, value objects, and bounded contexts.

answer.oneLiner: Entities have identity and a lifecycle; value objects are immutable and compared by value; an aggregate is a cluster of those with one root that guards invariants; a bounded context is the boundary where a model and its language stay consistent.
keyPoints: Entity — identity persists even as attributes change (e.g. Order)
Value object — immutable, equality by value (e.g. Money, Address)
Aggregate root — single entry point that enforces invariants; reference others by ID
Bounded context — one consistent model per boundary, often one per microservice
followUps: Why reference other aggregates by ID, not navigation?
How small should an aggregate be?
redFlags: Treating every table as an aggregate
Confusing entity with value object

questions.q: Event sourcing vs traditional CRUD — what's the difference and the trade-offs?

answer.oneLiner: CRUD stores only the current state and overwrites it; event sourcing stores an append-only log of every state change and rebuilds current state by replaying events, giving a full audit history at the cost of complexity.
keyPoints: CRUD — simple, current state only, history is lost on update
Event sourcing — append-only events are the source of truth
Benefits: full audit, time-travel, natural fit with CQRS read models
Costs: schema/versioning of events, replay cost, steep learning curve
followUps: What are snapshots in event sourcing?
Does event sourcing require CQRS?
redFlags: Using event sourcing for simple CRUD
Not knowing how to handle event schema evolution

questions.q: Caching strategies: cache-aside vs write-through vs write-behind — and where does Redis fit?

answer.oneLiner: Cache-aside has the app load and populate the cache on a miss; write-through writes to cache and DB together (consistent, slower writes); write-behind writes to cache and flushes to the DB asynchronously (fast, risk of loss). Redis is the common distributed store for all three.
keyPoints: Cache-aside — app manages it; lazy-loaded; most common default
Write-through — write cache+DB synchronously; strong consistency, slower
Write-behind — write cache now, persist later; fast but can lose data on crash
Redis serves as the distributed cache across instances for any of these
followUps: What is cache stampede and how do you prevent it?
Why is cache-aside the usual default?
redFlags: Thinking write-behind is safe without durability handling
No invalidation/TTL strategy

questions.q: Modular monolith vs monolith vs microservices — what are the missing distinctions?

answer.oneLiner: A modular monolith is a single deployable with strongly enforced internal module boundaries — it gives you the maintainability of microservices without the distributed-systems and deployment overhead.
keyPoints: Traditional monolith — one deploy, but often tangled (big ball of mud)
Modular monolith — one deploy, but clean module boundaries + in-process calls
Microservices — independent deploy/scale, but network + ops complexity
Modular monolith is the recommended starting point; extract services later
followUps: How do you enforce module boundaries in a monolith?
How does it ease migration to microservices?
redFlags: Equating modular monolith with a plain monolith
Jumping to microservices with no proven need

questions.q: What are the main multi-tenant architecture approaches (DB-per-tenant vs shared schema)?

answer.oneLiner: On a spectrum of isolation vs cost: database-per-tenant gives strongest isolation but high cost; schema-per-tenant is a middle ground; a shared schema with a TenantId column is cheapest and most scalable but needs strict query filtering.
keyPoints: DB-per-tenant — best isolation/compliance, costly, harder to scale to thousands
Schema-per-tenant — moderate isolation, moderate operational cost
Shared schema (TenantId) — cheapest and densest, but risk of data leakage
Shared schema needs enforced tenant filters (e.g. EF global query filters)
followUps: How do you prevent cross-tenant data leakage in a shared schema?
How do you handle a noisy-neighbour tenant?
redFlags: Forgetting tenant filtering on shared-schema queries
Ignoring per-tenant scaling/cost trade-offs

questions.q: How do you maintain backward compatibility when evolving a public API?

answer.oneLiner: Make only additive, non-breaking changes where possible (new optional fields/endpoints), version the API when you must break, and deprecate gracefully with notice and a migration window before removal.
keyPoints: Additive changes are safe; removing/renaming/retyping fields breaks clients
Tolerant reader: clients should ignore unknown fields
Version (v1/v2) only for genuine breaking changes
Deprecate with headers/docs and a sunset timeline, never remove abruptly
followUps: What counts as a breaking change?
How do you communicate a deprecation?
redFlags: Renaming/removing fields in place
Breaking changes with no version bump or notice

questions.q: What is the Bulkhead pattern and what problem does it solve?

answer.oneLiner: Like watertight compartments in a ship, the bulkhead pattern isolates resources (thread pools, connection pools) per dependency so a single slow or failing dependency can't exhaust shared resources and sink the whole app.
keyPoints: Partition resources per dependency instead of one shared pool
A slow downstream consumes only its own quota, not everything
Complements circuit breaker and timeout in a resilience strategy
In .NET, configurable via Polly's bulkhead/concurrency limiter
followUps: How does bulkhead differ from circuit breaker?
How do you size each bulkhead?
redFlags: Confusing it with circuit breaker
Using a single shared pool for all dependencies

questions.q: Vertical vs horizontal scaling — how does the choice affect your design?

answer.oneLiner: Vertical scaling adds power to one machine (simple, but a hard ceiling and a single point of failure); horizontal scaling adds more instances behind a load balancer, which scales further but forces you to design stateless services.
keyPoints: Vertical — bigger box, no code changes, but limited and risky (SPOF)
Horizontal — more instances, near-limitless, but needs statelessness
Move session/cache out of process (Redis), avoid in-memory request state
Horizontal also enables rolling deploys and fault isolation
followUps: Why does horizontal scaling require stateless services?
Where do you put session state then?
redFlags: Holding session/state in-process while scaling out
Thinking vertical scaling has no ceiling

questions.q: How do you design for zero-downtime deployment (blue-green, canary, rolling, feature flags)?

answer.oneLiner: Deploy a new version alongside the old and shift traffic gradually or instantly with a fallback: rolling replaces instances batch by batch, blue-green swaps two full environments, canary sends a small % first, and feature flags decouple release from deploy.
keyPoints: Rolling — replace instances in batches; cheap, slower rollback
Blue-green — two environments, instant switch and instant rollback
Canary — route a small % to the new version, watch metrics, then ramp
Feature flags — ship code dark, turn features on independently of deploy
answer.detail: All of these require backward-compatible changes — especially DB migrations must work with both old and new code running at once (expand/contract pattern).
followUps: Why must DB migrations be backward compatible during rollout?
What is the expand/contract migration pattern?
redFlags: Breaking DB schema changes that old instances can't tolerate
No rollback plan
